I decided on the basis of endoscopic evaluation to replace the urethra with So, the discussion was this, if it is possible to change, to do a complete substitution of the urethane with the helium, or if it is possible to do only a sub-total substitution because during the operation I needed to evaluate if the quality of the last parts of the ureter, the pelvic ureter was good in terms of quality of tissue or not. On the basis of this I can modulate my operation. Moreover, there is another limitation that the patients have

The patient is positioned in the flank position, and the surgeon begins the operation by mobilizing the right colon. After medializing the colon, a thick and hard tissue is visible, making dissection challenging. The surgeon carefully dissects around the urethra, progressing step by step to isolate it despite the difficulty posed by the dense tissue.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to delicately dissect and visualize the ureter, freeing it from surrounding adipose tissue. The ureter is isolated completely to assess its length for potential replacement.

So there is a lot of sticky fat around the ureter. Yes. So imagine that this patient performed before the one operation. He maintained the double GI for a long period. And this is another point. The third point is that the patient's underwent Some months before one operation in the ureter, so the realization of stenotic area and one anastomosis, terminal anastomosis. So it's the tissue, as you know, it's not a normal tissue, the elasticity of the ureter is not perfect. and so the possibility to consider the opportunity for the patient to perform only one segmentalization and doing terminal-terminal anastomosis was not possible and so in any case the technique should be focused only considering the replacement of the urethra I think that with the different organs so also during the operation I also evaluated the possibility to do to replace the urethra with appendix or with gonadal vein but it's not possible to do

The surgeon changes the patient's position from flank to a position similar to that used for radical prostatectomy. The distal parts of the ureter are dissected, revealing its attachment to the common iliac artery. The surgeon carefully navigates around the iliac vein to prevent complications, highlighting the difficulty in dissecting the ureter due to its close proximity to major vessels.

The surgeon cuts the ureter below the iliac vessel, noting the change in tissue quality from rigid to soft. The ureter is soft, while the surrounding periureteral tissue remains rigid. The surgeon removes fat around the ureter to prepare for anastomosis, ensuring it involves only the ureteral tissue to prevent stenosis.

The surgeon evaluates the distance from the ileocecal valve and places the first suture approximately 15 to 20 centimeters from it to mark the starting point for suturing the proximal part. The ileum is maintained in its physiological position to ensure accurate measurement and avoid tissue stretching. A stitch is placed at the marked point, and the distal part of the ileum is suspended using a monofilament suture.

The surgeon positions the ileum in its physiological site and evaluates its real length to avoid unrealistic measurements. The ileum is isolated and cut, then placed in front of the defect area to restore continuity with sutures.

The surgeon moves forward with the helium and cuts the corner of the distal parts of the helium that was previously cut. Using a stapler, the surgeon restores the continuity of the helium with a standard technique, employing a GIEA stapler with a 4.5 to 6-centimeter length. Two cuts are made to ensure sufficient continuity of the bowel, with delicate handling to avoid trauma.

The surgeon suspends the ileum to achieve proper alignment of its lateral face, ensuring only the ileum is involved in the suture. The ileum is rotated to avoid including other tissues in the suture. The surgeon prepares the ureter by removing surrounding fat, revealing a clean ureter ready for lateral terminal anastomosis. The procedure is performed in the Trendelenburg position, facilitating the dissection of the mesentery and peritoneum for better mobility of the ileum.

The surgeon removes the last part of the ileum, ensuring the anastomosis is terminal. Metallic clips are visible in the last part of the ileum, which could pose a risk for stone formation if urine drainage is imperfect. A single running suture is used, with barbed sutures applied during the neobladder configuration.

I switch, when I use, when I perform some radical cystectomy, but I always have some concern about the oncological aspects, and so the indications for me are very, very confined, so it means that usually I use this technique only when the disease is confined to the blood so it means if there are suspect infiltration of the fat or there are big tumors or suspect positive lymph nodes I think that the robotic approach is not a good indication because the risk to have a seeding to have a metastasis so the oncological safety in my opinion is not excellent so I think that you need to apply different principles, oncological principles for urinary tumors, for transitional tumors, for kidney tumors, for prostatic cancer and this is a tumor that we need to respect in terms of oncological point of view, in terms of safety because The risk of recurrence is very high. If you have recurrence, the possibility to save the patients and to treat the patients in a radical way is very, very low. So there is a very big risk of mortality. So this is one procedure. that is very important for patients because they can care for the patients and so this is the reason why we need to select the best procedure for him.

The surgeon repositions the patient in the flank position and begins cleaning the upper parts of the ureter up to the pelvis. The complete ureter is removed, and the surgeon assesses the elasticity of the ureter tissue before proceeding with the anastomosis. Fat is removed from the ureter, and its elasticity is tested to determine the optimal site for cutting and cleaning.

The surgeon removes fat from around the pelvis to ensure good mobilization for a tension-free anastomosis. The ureter is dissected, and the kidney is moved down slightly to further reduce tension during the procedure.

It's a very tricky operation because it's not very common and I think that there are not so many anatomical landmarks to calculate the length of this, the distance of that. Moreover, there are also the functional implications and it's very important to discuss about it because Obviously, you have to consider that the helium continues to produce mucous, first of all. The second one is the passage of the urine along the helium can give the possibility to have the reabsorbation. and so there are also the metabolic implications like acidosis and so it's important to follow the patients in the post-operative period but also at home because especially patients with chronic kidney disease like in this case because the creatinine was at one point eight before the operation and age of her was forty five so you can imagine that metabolic implications are very very very very important how do you suggest to follow the patient from this metabolic point of view as an example we used to do hemogastroanalysis to evaluate the base excess yes it's important to assess on the electrolyte every day second the hemogastroanalysis second is sufficient venous high mucous analysis and obviously it's important to support with the bicarbonate with alkalinism the patients in the post-operative period. So at this point, when I removed the Double J after fifteen days, we noticed that the passage of the urine from the urethra in the bladder was excellent, because in the first period when the Double J was in place, we had fifty percent of the drainage of the urine inside the bladder, and the fifth in the pylostomy.

When we removed the double J, ninety percent of the urine passage was second in the bladder. Yes, it's very important, this observation, because now, as you can see, now I introduce the double J, obviously I imagine that the anastomosis, that is obviously seen which the size of the helium is, larger than the ureter, obviously could have some difficulties for guarantee the urine passage, but I was confident that the peristalsis was sufficient to overcome this area. in fact this my theory and then they are supported by the results because the patients after three months when we remove the pyelostomy the creatinine is one point seven and the drainage of the urine is perfect we have an auto dilatation of the pelvis

The surgeon uses a double J stent to protect the distal part of the anastomosis between the ileum and ureter, visible as a terminal-to-terminal connection. Metallic clips are removed, and the area is remodeled with sutures.

The surgeon demonstrates the good vascularization of the tissue and performs a running suture to reduce the cul-de-sac. The appendix is removed, showing it was not used for ureter substitution due to insufficient length.
